description:
Trigger: put() finds N>=2 pre-existing duplicate objects (e.g. from a previous crashed put), then destroy_object fails on handle k (e.g. CKR_OBJECT_HANDLE_INVALID, CKR_SESSION_HANDLE_INVALID, CKR_ACTION_PROHIBITED).

Bad behavior: secretx-pkcs11/src/lib.rs:436-438 iterates 'for handle in existing { let _ = session.destroy_object(handle); }'. If destroy fails on one handle the loop continues, but the doc comment at line 432-435 implies only one stale handle would be left. In reality, if destroy_object returns an error mid-loop, all remaining handles in 'existing' are still destroyed too (the loop continues unconditionally). Actually the code IS correct on that point — the loop does iterate every handle. Reread: yes, the loop continues. So this finding is partially incorrect and should focus instead on:

Real issue: destroy_object failures are completely invisible. If one or more old handles fail to destroy, the doc-promised invariant that 'next put cleans up duplicates' relies on NEXT put's find_objects returning those stale handles too. That works, but the current put has no way to log or surface 'I just left N stale handles on the token' to ops. The let _ = silently swallows the rv. Operators have no signal that token state is degraded.

Fix: if any destroy_object call returns Err, capture all rvs and either (a) log via ::log/tracing, or (b) include a 'left N stale objects' note in a returned warning. At minimum, count failures and emit a one-line tracing::warn!.
title: put() destroy_object loop silently leaks all subsequent stale handles on a single failure
